exemplar.title: How does GDP per capita influence CO2 emissions in Tanzania, Uganda, and Mozambique over the period 2000-2020, and how does this relationship affect their climate change strategies in each LEDC?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does GDP per capita influence CO2 emissions in Tanzania, Uganda, and Mozambique over the period 2000-2020, and how does this relationship affect their climate change strategies in each LEDC?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows a recognizable ESS IA structure with clear headings
Uses appropriate environmental and statistical terminology in most sections
Overall flow is logical, helping the reader follow the main argument
weaknesses: Sections sometimes merge and tables split across pages, disrupting organization
Narrative is occasionally wordy and informal, reducing concision
Works cited entries are inconsistently formatted with duplicated and incomplete references
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure, terminology and coherence are maintained at a satisfactory level but not throughout, and significant formatting and citation issues persist, fitting the 2-mark band for communication.

strengths: Research question is clear, specific, time-bounded and focused on GDP per capitaâs influence on COâ emissions
Introduction provides relevant environmental context for the three LEDCs
Describes connections between GDP growth, industrialisation and rising emissions
weaknesses: Contextual discussion is largely descriptive with limited critical or comparative analysis across the three countries
Omissions in explaining how existing policies mediate the GDPâemissions relationship
Policy component introduced in the research question is not fully integrated into the context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student presents a coherent, narrowly framed research question and supplies relevant environmental context, placing performance in the 3â4 band. However, the descriptive introduction lacks depth in contrasting the unique environmental challenges and policy influences in each LEDC, preventing a top-band mark.

strengths: Outlined a repeatable secondary-data method (data collection, plotting, regression)
Described a sampling strategy selecting three diverse LEDCs
Acknowledged ethical considerations of transparency and citation
weaknesses: Method lacks detail on data cleaning, reliability checks and statistical treatment
Sampling justification is superficialârandomisation process and rationale for three countries are not documented
Risk assessment is only outlined and omits structured evaluation of digital or socio-political risks
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The plan is repeatable and yields relevant data, aligning with the mid-band descriptor. However, insufficient detail on preprocessing, sampling justification and comprehensive risk assessment confines this to a solid 3/6.

strengths: Evaluates conclusion in environmental context by referencing economic and energy-mix factors
Identifies several strengths and weaknesses of the method
Suggests a meaningful extension via multivariate analysis
weaknesses: Discussion omits treatment of data uncertainty, reliability and overfitting concerns
Method evaluation is descriptive rather than fully discursive, lacking depth on limitations
Improvement suggestions are broad without specific details on variables, sample sizes or tests
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation covers key areasâvalidity of conclusions, method strengths/weaknesses and further researchâbut omissions in depth and specificity place this in the 3-mark band for discussion and evaluation.

strengths: Appropriate use of scatter plots with regression lines for most data
Correct basic calculation of RÂ² values and qualitative interpretation
Insightful discussion of anomalies and country-specific factors
weaknesses: Charts have missing labels, illegible axes and omitted years (2018â2020)
Data analysis lacks statistical significance testing and comparative analysis across nations
Conclusion is only partially supported by specific quantitative data and units are not clearly stated
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student constructs mostly appropriate diagrams and provides a basic analysis, errors in labeling, incomplete statistical treatment and a partially supported conclusion limit performance to the mid-band level (3/6).

strengths: Proposes application of findings to inform sustainable development policies
Links proposed solutions qualitatively to the studentâs data and trends
Acknowledges some strengths (emissions reduction) and weaknesses (costs) of solutions
weaknesses: Justification remains general and lacks direct ties to specific country results
Evaluation of solution strengths/weaknesses is not exhaustive or deeply analysed
No detailed plan for implementing or assessing the proposed policy changes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes a plausible application and touches on solution pros and cons, matching the 2-mark descriptor, but stronger, data-linked justification and a fuller evaluation are missing.

strandSelections.reasoning: Environmental and statistical terminology (GDP per capita, RÂ², linear regression) is mostly correct, yet narrative is wordy and sometimes informal.  Either concision or terminology is consistently met, not both â band 2.

The student evaluates the validity of conclusions by discussing economic context and energy mix but omits depth on uncertainty or data reliability.  The evaluation is present with omissions, matching band 3-4, lower end.

The conclusion summarises the differing relationships and acknowledges complexity, referencing the data qualitatively.  Support from quantitative findings is partial, and influence on climate strategies is only briefly touched, giving a partially supported conclusion.

Basic linear regression, RÂ² values and qualitative interpretation are correct but limited; no statistical testing of significance, no comparative analysis between nations, and errors in reading R value vs RÂ².  Analysis is therefore correct yet incomplete.

The suggestion of multivariate analysis and inclusion of other variables is meaningful, yet only one broad modification is offered and no detailed, targeted improvements to data collection are provided.  Fits mid-band performance.

A repeatable secondary-data method (data collection, plotting, regression) is outlined, but details of statistical treatment, data cleaning and reliability checks are thin.  It should still yield relevant data, yet not âsufficientâ for the policy sub-question; thus mid-band 3-4 fits.

Connections between GDP, emissions and national strategies are described (e.g., higher GDP â industrialisation â rising emissions), yet linkages are not fully explained for Mozambique and Uganda and omit discussion of the policy component promised in the RQ.  Some omissions keep the strand in the middle of the 3-4 band.

The student states a clear, specific and time-bounded question focused on the influence of GDP per capita on CO2 emissions in three named LEDCs between 2000-2020, including an applied component on climate-strategy implications.  The wording is coherent and narrowly framed enough to be investigated with the data selected; only slight breadth creep (adding policy angle) prevents the full 6 marks.

Strengths (e.g., emissions reduction) and weaknesses (high upfront cost) of proposed solutions are mentioned but not exhaustively evaluated.  Some relevant factors are considered, matching the 2-mark descriptor.

The report follows a logical ESS IA structure with clear headings, but sections occasionally merge and tables split over pages; thus structure is present but not sustained flawlessly, placing it in band 2.

A table lists strengths and weaknesses of the approach and mentions oversimplification and missing variables.  Limitations are identified but not deeply explored, fulfilling a descriptive rather than fully discursive treatment.

The introduction describes climate change, CO2 as a greenhouse gas, and provides some facts about the three countriesâ development paths and existing policies.  This supplies reasonable context but the discussion is brief and largely descriptive, stopping short of a critical / comparative discussion of the environmental issue, so the work fits best at the top of the 3-4 band.

The student names simple random sampling and explains why the three countries give diversity, but justification is superficial (no evidence randomiser was used, no rationale for sample size of three).  Description rather than justification â lower half of 3-4 band.

Ethical section cites transparency and accurate citation but omits physical, digital or socio-political risks; no structured risk matrix is provided.  This constitutes an outline rather than a comprehensive description, therefore 3 marks.

Tables of raw data and three scatter plots with regression lines are appropriate and mostly labelled.  Minor omissions (missing some years, axes units small on images) prevent full completion but satisfy âappropriate diagrams of most relevant dataâ.

The student proposes applying results to guide sustainable development policies and outlines specific actions.  Justification links to findings but remains general and qualitative, aligning with band 2 (described with weak justification).

Arguments generally flow, but some paragraphs are repetitive and data is occasionally referenced after interpretation, causing minor difficulty in following.  The work is mainly logical but not fully coherent, fitting band 2.

annotations.comment: Research question is clear, specific and time-bounded, focusing on GDP per capitaâs influence on COâ emissions; the added policy element slightly broadens scope and could be reserved for the discussion.
Introduction frames climate change and COâ emissions descriptively but lacks critical, comparative analysis of how drivers differ across LEDCs; deepen contextual critique.
Country context paragraph outlines development paths but stops short of contrasting each nationâs unique environmental challenges; expand with comparative details.
Connections between GDP and COâ emissions are outlined but omit how existing policies mediate those relationships; integrate policy influence explicitly.
This table only shows Tanzaniaâs policies and omits Uganda and Mozambique despite mention in text; include all case-study countries or restructure the table.
Controlled variables are limited to region and LEDC status; consider including population size and energyâmix controls to reduce confounding variables.
Data source description is appropriate but lacks details on data cleaning, handling of outliers and missing values; specify preprocessing steps for replication.
Hypothesis states expected relationship clearly but introduces policy recommendations prematurely; reserve strategy discussion for the evaluation section.
Sampling strategy claims simple random selection but fails to document randomisation process and justify why three countries suffice; clarify method and rationale.
Method steps are listed comprehensively but omit statistical significance testing, error estimation, and robustness checks; integrate these to strengthen analysis.
Ethical considerations focus exclusively on citation practices; expand to address data source bias, digital security and potential misuse of findings.
Raw data table is overly dense and difficult to read; break into separate tables per country or apply filters to improve clarity and prevent misreading.
Second presentation of raw data lacks consistent units and misses labels for years 2018â2020; ensure uniform year columns and axis descriptors across tables.
Regression interpretation misstates slope as 13.8 kt per US$ without clarifying units; explicitly note units are kt per US$ and verify computed slope and intercept.
Scatter plot axes are unlabeled or illegible; add clear titles and units (GDP per capita in US$, CO2 emissions in kt) and enlarge fonts for readability.
Comment on Mozambiqueâs low RÂ² value is valid; reinforce by defining thresholds for weak vs strong correlations and comparing across all case studies.
Anomalies discussion is insightful; strengthen by pinpointing specific years and quantifying deviations to illustrate irregularities explicitly.
Uganda analysis effectively links sectoral drivers to emissions; consider summarizing country-specific factors in a table for clearer cross-case comparison.
Acknowledging imperfection of RÂ² is good; extend by discussing its implications for model reliability and potential overfitting concerns.
Conclusion captures general dynamics but lacks citation of specific data points; integrate key statistics (e.g., RÂ² values, slopes) to substantiate claims.
Strengths/weaknesses table is comprehensive but omits discussion of data uncertainty and reliability; add an entry addressing data quality limitations.
Multivariate analysis proposal is apt; enhance by specifying candidate variables, proposed sample sizes, and statistical tests to be employed.
Application suggestions connect findings to policy but justification for Mozambiqueâs regulations is broad; tie specific results to concrete regulation types.
Works Cited entries are inconsistently formatted and lack standardized style; apply a single citation format (e.g., APA) and include complete publication details.
UNCTAD citation lacks author and date details, and styling is inconsistent; supply full metadata and harmonize formatting with other references.
The final World Bank GDP per capita citation is duplicated; consolidate repeated entries and alphabetize for coherence in the works cited.
Trading Economics GDP citation repeats in two entries; merge into one and ensure consistent presentation to improve reader navigation.
